1: nāqᵉṭāh napšî bᵉḥayyāy ʾeʿezbāh ʿālāyw śîḥî ʾᵃḏabbēr bᵉmar napšî — “my soul is sick of my life; I will give free rein to my complaint; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.” Nāqᵉṭāh napšî (my soul loathes/is sick) — the verb qûṭ carries the sense of being cut off or disgusted; cf. Genesis 27:46 (Rebekah’s loathing of life over Esau’s wives). ʾeʿezbāh ʿālāyw śîḥî — “I will release/let go my complaint upon him” — the idiom of dropping one’s restraint; Job’s address to God becomes direct from this verse forward.
2: ʾomar ʾel-ʾĕlôhah ʾal-taršîʿēnî hôḏîʿēnî ʿal-māh tᵉrîḇēnî — “Do not condemn me; let me know why you bring charges against me.” Taršîʿēnî (condemn/declare wicked) is the legal opposite of ṣādaq (declare righteous); Job requests both an acquittal and an explanation. The request for an explanation — “let me know why” — is the central question of the book, and the whirlwind speech conspicuously does not answer it directly.
3: hăṭôḇ lᵉḵā kî-taʿᵃšōq kî-timʾas yᵉgîaʿ kappêḵā — “does it please you to oppress, to reject the work of your own hands?” Yᵉgîaʿ kappêḵā (the work of your hands/the labor of your palms) appeals to the creator’s investment in the creature; the same phrase appears in Psalms 128:2 (you shall eat the fruit of the labor of your hands) and Job 138:8 (do not abandon the work of your hands). The theological force: God has a stake in what God made; destruction of the creature undermines divine creative purpose.
4: hăʿênê ḇāśār lāḵ ʾim-kirʾôṯ ʾĕnôš tirʾeh — “do you have eyes of flesh? Do you see as a human being sees?” The question is anthropological: divine perception versus human perception. The ʿênê ḇāśār (eyes of flesh) are eyes that are limited, temporal, fallible; YHWH’s eyes are not — yet Job is asking whether God is operating as if he has these limitations, judging by surface appearances like a human being. This is the problem that Job’s friends have: they see exactly as human beings see, drawing human inferences.
5: hǎkîmê ʾĕnôš yāmeḵā ʾim-šᵉnôṯeḵā kimê gāḇer — “are your days like those of a human being, or your years like a man’s years?” The time-limitation question: if God’s days were finite, the urgency to condemn Job before evidence emerges would make sense. But God is eternal — there is no rush; no expiry date on investigation. The argument is that divine eternity should enable divine patience, which the present situation appears to contradict.
6: kî-ṯᵉḇaqqēš laʿᵃwōnî ûlᵉḥaṭṭāʾṯî ṯiḏrôš — “that you search out my wrongdoing and inquire into my sin.” Ṯᵉḇaqqēš (search for/seek) and ṯiḏrôš (inquire into/seek out) together describe intensive forensic investigation. The irony Job notes: God is applying diligent prosecutorial resources to find Job’s guilt, but God knows (Job 10:7) there is no guilt to find.
7: ʿal-daʿtᵉḵā kî-lōʾ ʾerāšaʿ wᵉʾên miyyāḏᵉḵā maṣṣîl — “though you know I am not guilty, and no one can rescue me from your power.” The theological crux: God’s omniscience includes knowledge of Job’s innocence, yet the treatment continues. The omniscience-and-suffering tension is not resolved by Job but is implicitly resolved in Job 42:7-8 when God validates Job’s case retroactively.
8: yāḏeḵā ʿiṣᵉḇûnî wayyaʿᵃśûnî yaḥaḏ sāḇîḇ wattᵉḇallᵉʿēnî — “your hands shaped and formed me — the whole of me — and now you turn and destroy me.” Two verbs of craftsmanship: ʿāṣaḇ (fashion/form/shape — used of potters, sculptors, image-makers) and ʿāśāh (make/do — the general creation verb of Gen 1); together they emphasize deliberate, skilled construction. Yaḥaḏ sāḇîḇ (altogether/all around) — God shaped every aspect of Job. The contrast with “swallow/destroy” (bālaʿ) is stark: the same hands that made now apparently consume.
9: zᵉḵār-nāʾ kî-kaḥōmer ʿāśîṯānî wᵉʾel-ʿāpār tᵉšîḇēnî — “remember that you formed me like clay; will you now bring me back to dust?” Kaḥōmer (like clay) — the potter-clay metaphor of Genesis 2:7 (formed from the dust of the ground) and Isaiah 64:8 (we are the clay, you are the potter). The appeal to clay-formation is simultaneously an argument for divine responsibility and for divine tenderness: clay is fragile; the potter should remember what the clay is made of.
10: hălōʾ kaḥālāḇ tattiḵēnî waggᵉḇînāh taqpîʾēnî — “did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?” The embryological analogy — liquid becoming solid, shapeless becoming shaped — is the OT’s most specific description of fetal development; cf. Psalms 139:13-15 (woven in the womb). Ancient physiology understood conception as a process of coagulation of vital fluids; the poet uses this to describe the deliberateness and intimacy of divine creative involvement in human formation.
11: ʿôr wāḇāśār talḇîšēnî ûḇaʿᵃṣāmôṯ wᵉḡîḏîm tᵉśōḵᵉḵēnî — “you clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.” Tᵉśōḵᵉḵēnî (knit/weave together) — the weaving metaphor for skeletal formation connects to Psalms 139:13 (tᵉsukkanî — you wove/knitted me). The body described as a garment put on (talḇîšēnî — clothed me) positions the physical form as given by God, not inherent to the self — a garment that can be removed but was given by the same hands now apparently destroying it.
12: ḥayyîm wāḥeseḏ ʿāśîṯā ʿimmāḏî ûpᵉquddāṯᵉḵā šāmᵉrāh rûḥî — “you gave me life and steadfast love (ḥeseḏ), and your watchful care preserved my spirit.” The attribution of ḥeseḏ (covenant steadfast love/loyalty) to God’s past action is theologically central: Job is not denying that God has shown covenant faithfulness — he is in crisis because the present experience contradicts the past. The tension between remembered ḥeseḏ and present silence is the spiritual anatomy of the book’s lament.
13: wᵉʾēlleh ṣāpantā ḇilḇāḇeḵā yāḏaʿtî kî-zōʾṯ ʿimmāḵ — “yet these things you hid in your heart; I know that this was your purpose all along.” Ṣāpantā ḇilḇāḇeḵā (you hid in your heart) — Job suggests that the suffering was a predetermined divine intention, not an improvised punishment. The “purpose all along” reading gives Job’s theology a hidden-plan quality — divine concealment of intent that the reader knows was the testing of chapters 1-2.
14: ʾim-ḥāṭāʾṯî wᵉšāmartanî wᵉmeʿᵃwônî lōʾ ṯᵉnaqqēnî — “if I sin, you take note of it and will not let my guilt go.” The relentless divine observation of sin (šāmar — keep watch) is experienced by Job as prosecutorial surveillance rather than protective care; the same word is used for protective keeping in Psalms 121:4 (“he who keeps Israel will neither slumber nor sleep”). Divine watchfulness that ought to be comforting has become threatening in Job’s experience.
15: ʾim-rāšaʿtî ʾallay lî wᵉṣāḏaqtî lōʾ-ʾeśśāʾ rōʾšî — “if I am wicked, woe to me! And if I am righteous, I still cannot hold my head up.” The double bind: the wicked are condemned; but the righteous cannot raise their heads either, because of the present humiliation. ʾAllay lî (woe to me!) is a self-directed curse expressing utter misery. The logical structure — wicked: condemned; righteous: still condemned — is the impossible situation Job is in.
16: wᵉyiśgeh kaššaḥal tᵉṣûḏēnî — “but if I do hold my head up, you hunt me like a fierce lion.” Šaḥal (fierce lion) is used in Psalms 91:13 for the lion the protected person treads upon; here it describes God as hunter. The inversion of the divine hunter-and-helper imagery is part of Job’s theodicy vocabulary — God as adversary rather than ally, using the strongest possible animal metaphors for power.
17: tᵉḥaddeš ʿēḏeḵā nᵉḡḏî — “you bring fresh witnesses against me.” ʿēḏeḵā (your witnesses) — God as the one who produces witnesses for the prosecution rather than defending the accused. The Hebrew legal system required multiple witnesses for conviction (Deuteronomy 17:6); the image of God as the one continuously producing fresh witness-evidence against Job is the legal metaphor for the continuous suffering Job experiences.
18: wᵉlāmmāh mērāḥem hôṣēʾṯānî mibbeṭen — “why then did you bring me out of the womb?” The birth-curse and wish-never-to-have-been pattern echoes Job’s opening speech in chapter 3 and connects to Jeremiah’s lament (Jeremiah 20:14-18). The question “why bring me out of the womb?” is not suicidal but theodical — if this is what divine care looks like, the initial act of bringing to life appears to have been cruelty rather than kindness.
19: kaʾᵃšer lōʾ-hāyîṯî ʾihyeh mibbeṭen laqqeḇer ʾûḇāl — “I should never have existed; I should have been carried straight from the womb to the grave.” The theological coherence of womb-to-grave without intermediate life — bypassing the suffering — is the impossible wish that echoes Ecclesiastes’s stillborn as better off than the living (Ecclesiastes 6:3-5). The OT allows lament to reach these extremes; Job’s hyperbole is authentic suffering speech, not theological confession.
20: hălōʾ-miʿaṭ yāmay yāḥᵃḏāl wᵉšîṯ mimmennî wᵉʾaḇliḡāh mᵉʿāṭ — “are my days not few? Cease — leave me alone, that I may have a little relief before I go.” ʾAḇliḡāh mᵉʿāṭ (a little brightness/relief) — the verb bālag means to shine/gleam; the request for a brief brightening before death is the minimum request, not a demand for restoration. The mᵉʿāṭ (a little) acknowledges the terminal expectation.
21: bᵉṭerem ʾēlēḵ wᵉlōʾ ʾāšûḇ ʾel-ʾereṣ ḥōšeḵ wᵉṣalmāweṯ — “to the land of darkness and the shadow of death, from which there is no return.” Ṣalmāweṯ (shadow of death) — a compound of ṣēl (shadow) and māweṯ (death); used in Psalms 23:4 (“though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death”), Psalms 107:10, and Isaiah 9:2 (“the people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of the shadow of death a light has dawned”). The darkness that Job anticipates entering is the darkness from which Isaiah promises a great light emerges.
22: ʾereṣ ʿêpāṯāh kaʾᵃpēlāh ṣalmāweṯ wᵉlōʾ sᵉḏārîm wattōpiaʿ kaʾōpel — “a land of utter gloom like thick darkness, of the shadow of death and disorder, where even the light is like darkness.” The description of Sheol as a place of disorder (lōʾ sᵉḏārîm — without order/arrangements) and inverted light (light there is like darkness) echoes the pre-creation chaos of Genesis 1:2 (formless and void, darkness over the deep). Job’s Sheol is a return to uncreation — the undoing of the cosmic order God established.
